title: White bloomer
subtitle: Free-form oval loaf with diagonal cuts
excerpt: A 700 g free-form white loaf with five diagonal slashes across the top, baked on a tray with an initial blast of steam. The crust is thick and chewy; the crumb open and white. The shape and cuts are what give a bloomer its character.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the white loaf chapter describes free-form oval loaves baked on a flat tin, with cuts made on the surface before baking to allow expansion. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the household bread section documents the bloomer shape and the importance of slashing the surface to control oven spring. The diagonal cut pattern and the name 'bloomer' are documented in British domestic baking manuals of the mid-nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze in a bag. Slices toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping, place on the lined tray, cover and cold-prove overnight. Bake straight from the fridge, adding 5 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, upper-middle shelf. Spray the oven walls with water immediately before loading to create steam. After 10 minutes reduce to 200°C and finish without steam

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting the surface of a shaped loaf with a sharp knife or lame immediately before baking. The cuts weaken the crust in controlled places so the loaf expands along the score lines rather than bursting unpredictably at the sides. For a bloomer, five diagonal cuts across the top produce the characteristic fanned shape.

content.text: A bloomer is a free-form oval loaf, baked on a flat tray rather than in a tin, with five diagonal cuts scored across the top before it goes in the oven. The cuts are not decoration: they control where the loaf expands. Without them, the crust sets before the crumb has finished expanding and the loaf tears at the weakest point. With them, the loaf blooms along each cut into the fanned pattern that gives it the name.

The dough is a straightforward white bread dough at 68% hydration. This is wetter than a sandwich loaf but not so wet that it spreads when free-formed. A small amount of oil in the bowl during the rise makes the shaping easier.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl, keeping the yeast and salt on opposite sides. Pour in the {{water}} and mix to a shaggy dough. Tip onto the surface and knead for 10 minutes by hand until smooth, elastic, and only slightly tacky. The dough should pass the stretch test: a small piece pulled gently should stretch thin before tearing. Lightly oil the bowl with {{rapeseed-oil}}, return the dough, and turn to coat.
Cover and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes until doubled. The dough should feel airy when you press it and spring back slowly.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface and knock it back briefly. Flatten into a rough rectangle, fold the long sides into the centre, then roll the dough away from you into a tight oval log about 25 cm long. Pinch the seam closed and place seam-side down on the tray.

Cover loosely with oiled clingfilm and leave for 45 minutes until visibly puffed but not fully doubled. Preheat the oven to 220°C fan with a baking tray on the upper-middle shelf. Fill the spray bottle with water.
Using a sharp knife or razor blade, make five diagonal cuts across the top of the loaf at 45 degrees, each about 1 cm deep. This is the

that controls where the loaf expands. Slide the tray into the oven, spray the oven walls with water, and close the door immediately. After 10 minutes, reduce the temperature to 200°C and continue baking for a further 18–20 minutes until the crust is deep brown and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for at least 30 minutes before cutting — the crumb continues to set as it cools.
